# Theodore Postol — Trump ceasefire, Netanyahu, Iran war, missiles & drones (digest)

**Source:** Long-form interview (host + **Theodore Postol**, MIT). Stated on tape: **Friday 10 April 2026**. **Watch URL:** *pin `https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=…` when known.*

**Ingested:** 2026-04-10 (operator paste).

**Participants:** **Host**; **Theodore Postol** (guest).

**Scope:** [work-strategy](../../../../docs/skill-work/work-strategy/README.md) upstream — opinion, technical analysis, and **charged political/moral language**. **Not** companion Record. Treat **poll percentages**, **RUSI** interceptor dates, **oil prices**, **order-of-battle**, and **combat** claims as **speaker-sourced** until triangulated. Some passages use **Hitler** analogies and **dual-loyalty** framing — **handle with care** in any public or ship-facing copy; do not strip context when reviewing in WORK.

---

## Perceiver — neutral summary (≤200 words)

Postol discusses Trump’s **two-week ceasefire** to negotiate an end to the **Middle East** war, **Israeli** unhappiness, and **John Kerry’s** past dealings with **Netanyahu**; he claims Netanyahu repeatedly sought **U.S. strikes on Iran** and that **Obama, Biden, and Bush** refused while **Trump** agreed. He argues Netanyahu’s aim is to **destroy Iran as a functioning state** and foster **fragmentation** (analogy to **Syria**), **divide-and-conquer** in southwest Asia, and reviews **1953** and **Mossadegh**, **JCPOA** merits, and **Trump’s** withdrawal as repeating **Netanyahu**. Extended **condemnation** of **Netanyahu** and **Israeli** society (**fascist** framing, **psychopath** language, **Hitler** comparisons); advocacy to **cut U.S. aid**; **poll** claim (~**70%** Americans against the war). **Military-technical** segment: **Iron Dome** **misuse** firing at **ballistic** targets; **interceptor** trails/slides; **RUSI** **late March** depletion **estimate**; **Iran** **drone** **precision**, **China/Russia** **satellite** reconnaissance, **radar** losses (**THAAD**, **Qatar**), **low-altitude** **drone** **clutter** and **radar** limits; **Starlink** on **drones**; **Hormuz**, **Iranian** oil priced at **~$120/bbl** (speaker claim); **nuclear** **use** **tail** **risk** warning. **All** numeric and strategic assertions require **independent** verification.

## Full transcript

*Light cleanup: speaker labels normalized to **Host** / **Ted Postol**; punctuation and obvious ASR fixes only. Substantive wording unchanged.*

**Host:** Hi everybody. Today is Friday, April 10th, 2026, and our dear friend and brother, Professor Ted Postol, is here with us. Welcome back.

**Ted Postol:** Nice to be here.

**Host:** Ted, let me start with what's going on with the war in the Middle East. We had the announcement from Donald Trump, the President of the United States, that there are going to be some sort of talks — two weeks of ceasefire to negotiate and do something about putting an end to the conflict. And it doesn't seem that Israel is happy with that.

But what is happening right now with Israel is not something new. Here is what John Kerry, former Secretary of State, said about Benjamin Netanyahu. He sat across the table from Prime Minister Netanyahu more than almost anyone else out there.

And Israel is of course a big factor here. There has been recent reporting, you probably saw in the New York Times, that Netanyahu was taken into the situation where he basically pitched Trump on attacking Iran. Were you ever a part of any conversations like that? I know he has made those pitches to past administrations. Were you ever a part of that? And were you surprised that he was able to convince Trump to do this?

**Ted Postol:** Well, I was part of any number of conversations with Prime Minister Netanyahu — conversations that took place in other countries — where he wanted the US to strike Iran. Yes, he wanted us to strike. He came to President Obama. He made a presentation to ask to strike. President Obama refused. President Biden refused. President Bush refused. The only president who has agreed to this, obviously, is President Trump.

**Host:** Ted, what is your understanding of what's going on right now?

**Ted Postol:** I'm probably not the best person to ask in terms of knowledge of the political subtleties, but I do have a view.

It appears by every measure and action Netanyahu has made, not only recently but over the years, that his objective — what he would like to do — is to destroy Iran as a functioning state. He would like to turn it into a failed state, a state of fragments that would be fighting among themselves, much like what he accomplished with the help of the United States — the incompetent help of the United States — in Syria.

What he wants to do is create a political vacuum in southwest Asia with regard to states that could potentially oppose Israeli military and expansionist objectives. So that once these states are no longer functioning, once they are failed states, then it's much easier to go about your business because it's the old divide-and-conquer mindset.

And that is pretty clearly what he is up to, what he has been up to for a long time now. Besides the issues of morality — which of course should be important, although in his case there's no detectable moral concerns of any kind that we can see in this man — but besides the question of morality, there's the question of practicality.

I mean, do you really want a failed Syrian state, for example, where we now have basically al-Qaeda in charge? It's laughable. Well, it's not laughable unfortunately. It's serious that the United States is trying to pretend that the current situation in Syria is better than having the situation in Syria where Assad was in charge. It doesn't mean that one thinks Assad is a good choice if one could make the optimal choice. But when you look at the world, you try to do your best and you try at least not to do more damage than has already been done, and you basically stay out of it if you can't do anything.

And of course, if you look at the history of American and also British involvement in Iran, it's the same situation. The situation in Iran is totally a product of American and British interventions in 1953. You had a democracy. Mossadegh was elected fair and square. And like any leader who wants to do what's right for his country — not for other countries that are looking to take an opportunity and take the resources that Iran has and steal them for themselves — Mossadegh wanted to nationalize the oil industry. He wanted to make sure that the Iranian people got a fair share of the wealth that was produced by the oil and the natural resources in Iran.

And what happened is the Americans and the British basically ran a campaign against him. They falsely accused him of being a communist — you know, all the normal operational procedures — and they destroyed him and they overthrew the government and they put a dictator in, the Shah, who was a very brutal dictator. And they gave him lots of things that probably they should not have, including a lot of support for a nuclear energy program, which was not really needed given the enormous natural fossil fuel-based resources that Iran had and still has.

But they created a program that Iranians rightfully are very proud of their contributions to. I mean, it's now a cultural thing. I was working with a colleague of mine in my research group, a wonderful man, Sir John Thomson, who was a British permanent representative to the UN, who unlike many of his colleagues was an honorable man. And when we talked about the Iranian nuclear program, he knew a lot about Iran and he had been involved in the Middle East. And he said, “Look, Iranians now have a great cultural pride in this nuclear energy program they have. You can't expect them to give it up.” That was his view. And he had all kinds of very intelligent ideas, really innovative ideas on how to help Iran develop this technology further while at the same time creating powerful incentives for Iran not to build nuclear weapons with the technology. Nobody would listen to him, but it was really amazing because it would have been good for Iran and it would have been good for the Western countries.

Then of course there was this tremendously negative development of the extremist Islamic elements taking control of the Iranian government. Iran is really quite a modern state. It has a long historical culture where education and professional standards have been very high, and of course the country was really not well suited for this quite repressive Islamic government. But that's what we got. But that was American behavior. That was a result of the American and British interventions.

Then we got this agreement on the enrichment of uranium that the Iranians agreed to. And it was a very good agreement in terms of constraining Iran from building a nuclear weapon. You mean the JCPOA which was signed by John Kerry?

**Host:** The Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action, the JCPOA.

**Ted Postol:** The Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action was absolutely airtight with regard to providing Iran with relief from sanctions — which was needed by Iran — and Iran for a short time got the benefit of that until we snapped back in a totally improper and unfair way. And the rest of the world got the confidence that Iran was working with nuclear technology but in a way that would not result in nuclear weapons. So it was a perfect agreement.

But then the Americans, in this case Donald Trump, just decided this agreement was no good. I mean, the interesting thing about Trump is he has these… Well, it's not a surprise. “Oh, it's a terrible agreement.” Well, explain it. Explain why it's a terrible agreement. I know quite a bit about the JCPOA.

I believe he was repeating what Netanyahu told him. He didn't know anything about it.

**Host:** Ted, when it comes to Netanyahu, because we had the attack on Lebanon. They don't like it. It doesn't seem Netanyahu likes it. What is your understanding of what's going on right now?

**Ted Postol:** Netanyahu is irrational. I think perhaps I don't know if he's mentally unstable or not. With Trump it's much clearer that he's mentally debilitated, but with Netanyahu there's something really wrong. The man is completely without any moral guide of any kind. He's a psychopath. He doesn't manifest it in exactly the same ways that Trump manifests it, but in terms of his moral commitment to anybody, including the Israeli people.

I mean, this man has destroyed Israel. He's turned Israel into an international pariah. And I don't want anything to do with the Israeli government. I'll never go back to Israel again. I just don't want anything to do with them. And he's played a major role in turning Israel into a functioning fascist state. It is a fascist state.

When you see the attitudes of a large fraction — not everyone, but a large fraction — of the Israeli population, they have become basically functioning fascists and their attitudes are criminal. I just saw an interview just yesterday. I had to show it to my wife because I was so upset by it. This woman was asked about killing children in Gaza and her response was, “Well, they grow up to be Arabs.” I mean, I just could not believe it. And there were a whole bunch of other interviews of this type in this particular video, and this appears to be a very common attitude.

It's not the attitude among my friends. I would not engage with people who have that attitude. But it's very common, and it's this kind of attitude that has led to the slaughter in Gaza and the carpet bombing of southern Lebanon now.

And this is all due to Netanyahu's political skills, which are substantial — although he's evil, and evil is the right terminology although it's oversimplified. But you have this evil man who's incredibly skilled politically. Incidentally, so was Adolf Hitler. I'm in Germany here and I have many friends, and of course World War II does come up in discussions. It's hard not to in a place like Germany. And I just last night was having a conversation with some friends and I just said, “Well, you know, Hitler was a very talented man.” And they kind of reacted, and I said, “Well, you have to — he was evil, but you can't ignore that these people can have skills, because it's these skills that, if they were just evil and they didn't have skills, they wouldn't be so dangerous.”

But Netanyahu only appears to be concerned about himself, although maybe he has a distorted belief in making this Israel into this greater Israel, whatever that is. But he has destroyed Israel not only in terms of undermining the basic society in a profound way. You can't get soldiers who are snipers to shoot children unless they think it's right. And when you see that kind of thing, that's beyond simple war crime. It has something to do with a sickness in the society that has somehow become acceptable.

You just can't have officers who oversee the snipers who are equally sick. In fact, they're worse. So these kinds of phenomena are just clear indications that the society has descended into some kind of fascist state — a very dangerous, murderous form of fascism and racist form of fascism.

Netanyahu is not alone. He's part of this group of people. Some of which I won't say I grew up with, but some of whose attitudes I had seen as a young child when I was in an immigrant Jewish community where these people would talk about Jews being the chosen people and somehow being superior. And even as a very young person, 11 or 12, I just found it objectionable. I don't know why, because a lot of people around me found no problem with it, but I just found it objectionable. And it carried on into my adulthood. I always found myself very critical of this dangerous strain, this thread in religious Jewish culture.

Now, that's not to say that all people have these ideas, but if you foster it and you encourage it and it takes over — as it has in Israel now — you get a state that's functionally a fascist state. And that's what we now see. And that's not just Netanyahu. He has been the orchestrator, but there was a lot there to begin with. It's just like in Nazi Germany. There was a lot there to create the monster that Hitler created in Nazi Germany. It wasn't just Hitler. He was very skilled. He was able to manipulate the society and the culture given the circumstances.

And now you have a similar situation in Israel and it's just horrifying for at least for me.

Now, the other problem that Israel has now gotten — and I hope there are Israelis listening to what I'm about to say — is that a very large percentage of Americans, and I would include American Jews, now see Israel as a fascist state, and they do not like that Israel is getting these tens of billions of dollars of support from the United States to continue doing this monstrous murder of people all around them.

And there are a lot of people, myself included, who are strong advocates for ending all aid from the United States to Israel. Now, if that happens — I don't think it will easily happen because of the Israeli lobby, this ultra-wealthy group of American Jews who are more loyal — and I'm going to say this in a way I wouldn't normally — who are more loyal to Israel than they are to the United States, which they are citizens of. If they feel so loyal to Israel relative to the United States, then I think they should renounce their American citizenship and go to Israel because this is unacceptable.

You know, I'm an American first. I'm a citizen of the world and I have responsibilities as a citizen of the world and I have responsibilities as an American citizen. And my country is not — if I have anything to do with it and I will try everything I can — my country will not become dominated by Israeli interests, Israeli monstrous and fascist and murderous interests, and to do the dirty work of a bunch of people who all should be nullified and either be in prison or be in a place where they can't do any harm to anybody else. It's just unacceptable to me as an American.

So Israelis who think that they're going to get a lot of help after this war is over in the form of unconstrained amounts of money — because it's basically unconstrained American gifts to Israel. We sell them arms, but then we have all these special programs which pay for the arms. So all these Israeli arms — they're not paying for these arms. They are being gifted to Israel.

So my position as an American citizen, if I could advocate a policy, a new policy in a new government — which I hope we'll be seeing because I think Trump is going to be thrown out, although it's not clear exactly when and how — I would advocate all aid to Israel be stopped. I think Israel has gone over the edge and they no longer should be further supported to do this murderous stuff that they are doing by the United States. So I would immediately stop all arms shipments to Israel immediately if I could. It won't happen right now. But if they have no bombs, they can't continue killing people in Gaza the way they've been doing. They can't continue carpet bombing Lebanon.

I'm not for the destruction of Israel, but it has to be reformed and it has to be a state that is a non-apartheid state if it's going to survive, if it's going to get my support as an American. And I want to be clear, I'm speaking as an American now. I forget the fact that I come from a Jewish background. Forget it. It's irrelevant. I'm speaking as an American. And this is unacceptable to me as an American. And I think there are a lot of other Americans who feel this way. It's no longer a minority.

And so those Israelis who think there's no negative side to this beyond what's already happening — which is of course negative enough — need to think about what the future is, because without the tens of billions of dollars of support for this country, it is not going to continue to survive and prosper the way it has.

So I think there are some big changes going to happen in the next years ahead. It's not going to happen instantly, but the reaction among American citizens right now is swinging very strongly against what is happening in southwest Asia. Very strongly. Some of these polls show 70% of Americans are against this war. They're not all overtly for it or against it, but to have no more than 25 or 30% of your citizens for the war — when 15% of them of course are these extremist Christian sects who think that when the end of the world occurs, it'll all happen with the end of the world coming in Israel — you are down to a very small percentage of people who are among thinking Americans when you look at the attitudes of the great majority of Americans.

And my greatest concern right now is as the pendulum swings — and it will swing, it is swinging — that people do things in a sensible way, that it not swing in a way that is too one-dimensional, that people not do things that are counterproductive toward bringing peace and stability to southwest Asia. But I think the American public is moving in that direction and I think we're going to see some big changes. And the question is whether the American political establishment is up to implementing those changes. And that I basically think of the young Democrats because the older Democratic establishment needs to be swept aside, and the Republicans who are decent, honest people who have been offended and recoil from what Trump and the Republican Party has become. There are a lot of Republicans who are very decent. They're conservative politically and that's fine. I have no problem with that. These people have to retake control of their party as well. And there has to be a cooperative move toward bringing the United States into a more stable configuration where it's not posing such a threat to the entire world, because the United States is posing this threat.

When we blindly provide Israel with the means to do what it's doing, we are part of the problem. There's no question about it.

So anyway, that's my reaction to the question of Netanyahu. He has to be stopped. He has to be thrown out of office. The Israelis are the ones who have to do it. I don't know if it's possible. I don't know the terrain of the politics in Israel. Israel is in big trouble. They have this extremist religious component in the country. It's been a problem for a long time. It's now become such a problem that it's taken over and the forces needed to retake control of the country are largely suppressed at this point and I don't know if things can swing in the other direction. I just don't understand the political landscape in Israel well enough. But if it doesn't happen in Israel, it has to happen from outside. And the United States is the outside player that can and should do this. And as an American, I'm going to do everything in my power to see that happens.

**Host:** Yeah, Ted, when it comes to the war militarily, looking at what has happened so far and you see this sort of willingness on the part of Netanyahu to continue this war, what is militarily in his mind? Because so far we've seen the latest photos, footages coming out — they're using interceptors that were produced in 2026 this year. So what is he planning for? What is happening in terms of the military operation and the strategic military operation against Hezbollah?

**Ted Postol:** If he understands the military situation — and I don't know how he wouldn't — but I know there are Israelis who do understand the situation. I mean, when I was involved in exposing the Patriot fraud during the war of 1991, I just met an enormous number of first-rate Israeli military people and I know these people understand the military situation when they see it. And they have to understand that the military situation for Israel is desperate.

Hezbollah has reconstituted itself in Lebanon and they are using a tremendous amount of drone technology now. They've adopted that technology for their combat purposes. They've destroyed a lot of Merkava tanks. You know, that's a fantastic tank. I mean, the Merkava is a really impressive piece of technology and they're being destroyed all over the place. You can't do what the Israelis have been doing in the past and lose all these tanks, and you can't have the Israeli Defense Forces taking casualties.

Israel is like the United States. The United States is very sensitive to casualties. In a place like Lebanon where you have an organization like Hezbollah, I won't say that they care less about human life — I'm not suggesting that at all — but they are in a struggle for what they see as their survival. They're right. This is a struggle for survival. When you're in a struggle for survival, you are willing to take enormously more casualties than you would otherwise be willing to take. And we see that. And they also, of course, they come from an Islamic culture where martyrdom has a special place in the culture.

If you talk about the Christian culture or the Jewish culture, being a martyr is also appropriate in appropriate circumstances. When the Israelis talk about fighting to the last person for their survival, they mean it, but they're not fighting to the last person for their survival at the moment. They're going out and murdering people who are largely innocent, who really would just want to be left alone. And there are a lot of Israelis who are not willing to lose their lives over it. If I were in Israel, I'd be doing everything I could to avoid being out in combat. I'd be willing to fight and die for my country, but I'm not willing to fight and die to murder people who pose no threat to my country.

And they're having a lot of problems. They call up people and a lot of them don't show. And that was unthinkable in Israel — the Israel I know from decades ago. You would never not show up if you were called up. You would be there. But you'd never shoot a child. That's also true at that time. So something has changed and it's for the sociologists and anthropologists to study this in the future and explain it. But whatever has changed, it's clearly the situation now. And something has to be done about it. And I think it's going to have to come from the outside right now. It's going to have to come from the United States. So that's where I'm focused.

But this guy Netanyahu — I don't think he knows what he is doing. I cannot imagine that anybody who is following the policies he is following and understands at any level what he's doing to Israel would care about his country. It's like Adolf Hitler turning Germany into a graveyard. Germany was a very powerful country when World War II was going on, but Hitler turned Germany into a graveyard. And that's what Netanyahu is doing. He's turning Israel into a graveyard.

And I feel horrified by it, but the only thing I know how to do is to try to influence American policies because we're the ones who are co-conspirators in this by allowing this to occur. So when Democrats start complaining to me about Trump I say, well look what Biden did. Biden, the first thing that should have happened after this terrible event of October 2023, he should have gone in there and he said, “You cannot respond the way you are talking about responding. You cannot go in there and murder all these innocent people. This is a terrible thing. You have to deal with it. But you cannot go out and do the same kind of murderous genocidal thing that happened to you.”

And by the way, a lot of your people killed a lot of Israelis. And you better look at that too because a lot of the Israelis who were killed in the incursion in October of 2023 were killed by Israeli forces firing indiscriminately at people who were taken prisoner. So it wasn't a simple situation, and we don't know all the details, but we do know some of them. And we do know that the Israeli Defense Forces played a significant role in the killing of Israeli citizens in addition to Hamas. Nobody's talking about that.

So this is all part of the campaign of misinformation trying to take these incredibly brutal policies of the current Israeli government and blame everybody else for it. And it doesn't wash with me and a lot of other Americans who are looking at the situation. And something has to be done and unfortunately it's going to take time. But hopefully it's going to happen. But a lot of unfortunate deaths are going to continue to happen until this thing is brought under control.

But Netanyahu has to be looked upon as — in terms of his behavior — it really is very much like a Hitler. I want to be clear about what I'm saying here. So when people react to what I'm saying, I want to be very clear what I mean here. Hitler showed no regard for the well-being of the German people. He murdered Germans as much as anybody else did. He was the one who caused the catastrophe in Germany. He was helped by a lot of people just as Netanyahu is being helped by a lot of people. But the moral judgment about how Hitler behaved and who he was applies just as well to Netanyahu as to Adolf Hitler. No question about it. And if someone wants to debate that with me, I'm happy to have that discussion. I'm not simply calling him a name. I'm talking about the moral behavior and his loyalty to his own people. He has shown himself to not care about the well-being of his own people and his own state and somebody in Israel should do something about it.

**Host:** That gets to the numbers on the battlefield, to the equipment, to the drones, to the way that this war goes on. What can you tell us about the situation?

**Ted Postol:** Well, I think the situation for Israel relative to Iran is going to continue to get worse and there are bunches of reasons for this. I can talk a little bit about the technology issues. And I'd like to do that. It's really not immediately relevant to the situation of the moment but it sheds light on it. So it's important to contemplate it.

If we go to the slides, for example, why don't we take slide two… there's been an extraordinary mismanagement by Israel of its air defenses. Let's go to the next slide. I'll show you that.

This slide is a time-lapse photo of Iron Dome interceptors being shot into the air. You can see tens of interceptors. Now what you see is the camera — the shutter is open for maybe 10 or 15 seconds. So as the rocket motor drives the interceptor along its trajectory, the rocket motor produces a point of light. If you were to do instant photographs, you would just see a point of light, but the point of light is moving. So it traces out a line.

So the lines you see are the trails of the interceptors as they are rising up because you had a bright point of light which, as it moves, produces a line which shows you the motion of the interceptor. Now you see these interceptors all are climbing up and then they're turning downward. They should never turn downward. The only way to have a chance of intercepting a ballistic missile target is to be flying up the expected trajectory.

So all of these — at the end of almost all of these lines you see little points of light. Those points of light are the warheads on the Iron Dome interceptors detonating because there's no target there for them to detonate on. So it's the interceptors flying up and just exploding in the air probably because the system wants to destroy the interceptor so you don't get a full interceptor falling to the ground because the ground damage is lower — still significant.

But now this should never have happened because those interceptors have no capability against ballistic missiles, but they are very capable against drones and cruise missiles. So this shows that the air defense operations were throwing away interceptors against targets that they had no possibility of hitting — zero chance of hitting — and instead you could have saved these interceptors for the drone attacks that are now occurring.

So this is the kind of mismanagement of the air defenses that I find astonishing. And the reason I say I find it astonishing is because a lot of the people I've known over the decades in Israel are incredibly capable people who worked on air defenses. I know how smart they are. So I can't understand it. I had a discussion with one friend of mine and he couldn't explain it to me because he said, “I'm not part of the operations these days,” but he says, “I agree with you. I have no idea. I have no understanding.”

So they've thrown away these interceptors and that's a real problem that the Israelis are facing.

Now the next slide shows the other problem that they should have anticipated but didn't. And this shows you several frames from a video of one of these Alpha ballistic missiles coming into the atmosphere, arriving at a fantastic speed. The speed of this warhead is probably Mach 10 or 11 or even higher.

Now the reason the speed is so high — if you look carefully, let's look at the upper left inset — you see something to the right of the main kind. That's a reflection off the lens of the camera. That's not real. That's just an artifact of the camera reflection from the bright part of the contrail to the left. But you see the front end of the contrail is very much brighter than the remaining part of the contrail. That's because there's a rocket motor thrusting this warhead forward against the drag of aerodynamic drag because the warhead would normally slow up and you'd expect it to slow up to maybe one and a half or two kilometers per second if you do the aerodynamic drag calculation. But the rocket motor keeps the speed high. And so when it hits the ground, it hits the ground at Mach 10 or higher.

Now if you look at the last slide to the right lower, you see the bright flash from the detonation and you see the sky is blue. And the reason the sky is blue is because the energy at the detonation point is so high that the temperature is higher than you would normally see from an explosion. If you look at most of the explosions on the ground when you don't have this highly energetic warhead coming in, the color tends to be red or yellow, which indicates a lower temperature at the peak of the explosive energy output. This is higher because you have the kinetic energy of the impact which contributes as much energy as the equivalent weight of high explosive.

So if there were no explosive in this warhead at all it would have the explosive energy of its weight in TNT but it also has TNT in it — high explosive. So you have twice the explosive density of what you would have from just TNT or just a kinetic impact. And this causes a much more violent explosion which causes much more damage at the aim point, at the target point.

And when you look at some of the damage areas — unfortunately I didn't have time to put it into this — you can see the effects of these much more energetic explosives. They are doing tremendous damage to very highly reinforced buildings.

But these two effects which have been going on now for a large part of the war — during the 40 days of this war — have resulted in the depletion of interceptors.

So if we look at the next slide, this is distilled from a Royal United Services Institute report that was published at the end of March. So basically a week and a half ago. This is their guess — I want to be clear it's a guess that they made based on their best estimates of interceptors being used up at the rate at which interceptors are currently being used up.

You can see that if you look at the last column, they're talking about all of the Iron Dome interceptors being used up by the end of April, maybe. That's not correct. I'm sure that we are nearly at the end of the Iron Dome interceptors being available and we're nearly at the end of the air-to-air missiles that could be fired from aircraft against drones. So these are the missiles that could be used against drones. The capabilities of intercepting ballistic missiles are near zero no matter what you have left. In other words, the Arrow 2 and Arrow 3 and David's Sling and THAAD have a very low intercept rate against the ballistic missiles. They've always had an intercept rate that's low.

So what you have indicated — we don't know for sure, but roughly speaking — is you have a depletion of the air defenses against drones and an ongoing inability to shoot down ballistic missiles. But the ballistic missiles are even more difficult to hit and even more damaging when they reach the ground. And they reach the ground at a higher rate of efficiency and the percentage of launched missiles reaching the ground is much higher. Not only because the intercept rates are low, but also because the missiles are more reliable. The technical features of them make them more reliable during re-entry. So the warheads essentially always detonate, which earlier was not always the case because the Iranians were firing older missiles where the warheads sometimes tumbled, broke up and sometimes did not detonate.

So the situation we're now facing is summarized in the next slide. And we are now being — the Israelis and the Americans. First of all, they've mismanaged their Iron Dome defense system. And that is serious because the Iron Dome has a very high effectiveness shooting down drones. But they've expended most of them and they will be out of Iron Domes against drones. And the drones are very very problematic as I'll explain.

The availability of timely and high-resolution satellite reconnaissance data is now available from China and Russia for Iran. So the Iranians have information on the location of air defense units which you try to move around — air defense radars — and thereby this allows them to target those radars with their drones.

And we saw a tremendously effective attack on the ballistic missile defense radars in the first two days of the war. That was effectuated by the strategic reconnaissance of the Chinese and the Russians that was given to the Iranians. The Iranians very cleverly — and I want to underscore here — Iran's military planning has been superbly well executed. There have been no mistakes that I can find — unlike the Israelis who I don't expect to make mistakes of the kind they've made with their air defenses.

I think what happened with their air defenses is they've been lying about the capability of Iron Dome against ballistic missiles and they became the victims of their own propaganda and just wasted all these interceptors against targets they had no chance of hitting and now they don't have interceptors to shoot targets that they do have a good chance of hitting. So that was a strategic blunder of a non-minor level.

So the Iranians have made no such blunders. So they had these extremely accurate drones and they were able to use them to destroy these fantastically expensive and small numbers of ballistic missile defense radars that the Americans and Israelis had. In particular, there were four THAAD radars. I have a picture of one of them later on in the discussion if we get to it. And a giant radar in Qatar that was critical for the overall defense of Israel against ballistic missiles. And this giant radar was critical because the giant radar could see pretty much everything that was launched from Iran when it was launched and then tell the smaller, less capable THAAD radars where to look for the incoming ballistic missiles. So these radars could then manage the defensive interceptors — the THAAD and Arrow 1 and Arrow 2 and David's Sling interceptors — because the radars operated by those systems were less capable in terms of range and ability to acquire large numbers of incoming warheads.

So Iran took that capability away from Israel and the United States literally in the first day or maybe two of the war. That was an amazing accomplishment. I did not expect it. I knew the drones were going to be a problem for the Americans and the Israelis, but I did not expect that precision in finding targets of great effect.

In other words, the satellites gave the Iranians the key data about exact locations of these radars, almost all of which could have been moved except for the big radar in Qatar. And it allowed the Iranians to put drones on these radars and they did it very quickly.

What I don't know, but I'm pretty sure is the case, is how many smaller air defense radars have also been destroyed because the Iranians have got information about those radars too, but it's not as assured because the radars are smaller. They're harder to locate, but they certainly can be located with some efficiency by the Chinese and the Russian reconnaissance satellites. And those radars are certainly also being destroyed. And you know that has to be the case because the air defenses at the American bases in the Persian Gulf have hardly been operating. You see videos of these drones coming in and no interceptors being fired at them. And some of it may be just simply because the Americans have run out of interceptors, which of course is an incredible oversight on the part of the Americans. And the other part could easily be because the radars to guide these interceptors no longer exist — they've been destroyed or damaged.

So the increasing losses of all these air defense radars is going to continue as the war goes on. And as the war goes on, the Iranian drones will have an easier and easier time to go after critical targets that the Americans and the Israelis have. So the drones don't have as large a warhead as you get with these ballistic missiles. So you might have a one-ton warhead on the ballistic missile whereas you have a one-tenth of a ton warhead on the drone. But you don't need 100-kilogram warheads if you have precision. If you want to bring down a building, a 100-kilogram warhead is more than enough if you can place it with meters of accuracy.

So the fact that the drones have a smaller warhead does not mean that tremendous damage cannot be inflicted. Plus of course the numbers of drones you can bring to bear against targets of interest is enormous — thousands, tens of thousands of these drones. And we know that the Iranians can continue producing these drones and they will be unstoppable.

So if we look at slide seven, I can begin to tell you how even if they can preserve the radars, it's going to be nearly impossible to stop the drones. So that's another factor. So, I'm not only saying that the drones are going to be more capable of doing damage because they have high accuracy and Iran has data from China and Russia that allows them to find the targets. So they can find the targets and they can hit them. But even when there are some radars that are preserved, the radars will not be able to find the drones because the drones are increasingly able to fly very low. And when they fly very low, they are in what's called the clutter — ground clutter.

So for example, when you talk about AWACS — airborne warning and control systems — aircraft-borne radars, those are going to be only of very limited capability against drones. So they've already destroyed at least one AWACS on the ground and there will be others probably destroyed as time goes on, but there will be some airborne warning and control system radars operating. Of course, they cannot operate 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. There may be five or six of these. So you can have them on a 12-hour mission. So you need maybe three aircraft to keep two on station 24 hours a day because it takes time to refuel and repair the aircraft.

But if we look at the next slide, we can see why those airborne warning and control systems are of very limited capability. First of all, the earth is round. And so there are two phenomena that are extremely important. If we look to the left, we see a radar, an icon of a radar dish. And the radar is looking straight out and there's a shadow. The Earth is curved so there's a shadow region below which the radar cannot see. And that shadow region is many tens of kilometers — if the airplane in fact it can be potentially a hundred kilometers out because the airplane is in the air like a radar warning and control system.

But notice there's an area called the clutter zone. And that clutter zone actually extends out for quite a distance and makes it basically not possible to see the drones. And I'm going to explain very briefly what clutter is because clutter is very important. This clutter hides the drone and makes it — even when the radar has a line of sight to it — not possible to see.

So if we go to slide nine, if we have an airplane that's flying at a high altitude, basically we can potentially see targets and we have a line of sight to the target even 50 or 100 kilometers away. But that's misleading because if we go to the next slide, we see what the clutter produces. We see the actual radar reflectivity of a drone. If we look at the left corner, we can see the upper left corner shows you the radar cross-section of an insect. That's about 1/1000 of a square meter. The next one shows you the radar cross-section of a drone is 1/100 of a square meter. That's really very small.

If you want to get a sense of how small it is, if we look below the drone radar cross-section, the radar cross-section of a bird is comparable. So how do you tell the difference between a bird and a drone? You know this is a problem. Now, it turns out there is technology for doing this, but the technology is limited in its precision. And so the bottom line is you cannot tell the difference. You cannot see the drone in the clutter.

And I'm going to explain what clutter is just so your audience has a rough sense of this. And the reason this is important is this is basically saying that even with the radar aircraft, the radar aircraft are not going to contribute to an air defense warning system against these drones. Of course, you're also going to run out of interceptors as well. So even if you have some interceptors, the radar system is not going to be able to guide those interceptors because you still need a radar for the air defense and those radars are being destroyed.

So if we go to the next slide, we see an illustration of what clutter is. So you're in an airplane and you're looking down at the surface of the earth and you illuminate a patch of the surface area of the earth. Now imagine that surface area acts like a perfect mirror. In that case, you would see no backscattered reflected signal. So if there were a radar reflection from a drone, you would get that signal. It would be a very very small signal because the radar cross-section is very small, but you would not get a competing signal from the illuminated ground.

But instead, think of a flashlight. Imagine you have a flashlight and you're in an open area and you shine the flashlight down on a mirror. At this angle, you would see no reflection from the mirror. So if you saw an insect flying above the mirror, you might actually see the insect above this black surface because you don't see a reflection. But imagine that the surface is made up of trees or of mountains or of rolling hills or of grass or whatever — then you're seeing a big reflected signal because you're illuminating a very large area relative to the area you're illuminating when you're looking at the drone.

So the drone is going to get hidden in the clutter and clutter comes from all kinds of sources. So the big problem is not simply seeing the small radar cross-section target — which is a gigantic problem by itself — but it's also seeing it against the interfering reflected signals from other sources.

So if we go to the next slide, we see that there are all kinds of contributions to clutter. You have weather clutter. You have rain. You have ground reflections. You have — if you look at the right and below — you see an aircraft in a shadowing region because the shadowing region can be not only caused by the curvature of the earth. It can be caused by objects between the radar — mountains or trees or whatever — and the target you're trying to see. All of that is eliminating your ability to see targets.

So if I go to the next two slides, if you see that this slide is just depicting birds and trees and things giving me false signals, interfering signals. If I go to the next slide, I can just see here's a radar target area. So if you look near the radar, there's all kinds of clutter from buildings or trees or whatever. Further out, you can have rain clouds, you can have echoes from buildings. You know, because you might have a set of buildings in some areas that sets up an echo. You can see one is called an urban spike.

So when the Iranians attacked the Abqaiq oil field in Saudi Arabia, the drones came in. In this case, they were not drones. They were cruise missiles. They came in over buildings. So you have these buildings. So I have this drone and here's the drone and it reflects a very small amount of radio signal and the building is reflecting a tremendous radio signal. So I'm trying to see this drone. It's a small signal from the drone against the radar reflection from the building.

So let me just give you one very simplified idea of how the radar tries to see the drone. So there are these very exotic radio technologies that are used. So if we go to slide 16, we see a depiction of how the radar tries to see. I mean the signal is tremendously large. So the radar in blue sends a signal out trying to see a low-flying drone. Well, the only thing that makes the drone reflection different from the reflection from the ground is that the drone is moving toward the radar. So the reflected signal shown in green is of a slightly higher frequency than the signal from the ground. So the ground is a tremendous signal.

So the processed signal shown in red here is a signal where I intentionally mix the signal I'm receiving with the low-flying signal from the target. And what I get is a beat frequency that's depicted here. So if I go to the next slide, I know the frequency of the signal. So for a drone, I know the signal is going to be about 300 hertz. That's going to be the shift in frequency. But the frequency of the radar is a gigahertz — a billion hertz. So I have to have a frequency stability. If the frequency of the radar is moving by a few hundred hertz, then I won't be able to see the 300 hertz frequency beat frequency. I'll be seeing a beat frequency, but it'll be just from the beat frequencies from my oscillating frequency.

So you can see how precise the radar signals — the primary signal — has to be controlled. So what I've shown in the next slide are the beat frequencies for one and two hertz changes in frequency. So if I have a one hertz change in the frequency I get this beat frequency of one hertz — it would be 100 hertz in this case. And if it's a two hertz you can see I get twice the beat frequency. And in the next slide I show that the beat frequencies are the same even if I — in this case I've assumed twice the frequency but the beat frequencies are the same. So no matter what the frequency of the radar I get the same beat frequency. The problem is if I use a higher frequency radar like an X-band radar which is a typical radar that I want to use for tracking — high precision tracking — then the frequency of the radar is 10 times higher which means I have to control the base frequency to even 10 times more.

So the net result is I really, when I look at the problem of controlling the frequency of the radar and also controlling the frequency changes because it's an airborne radar — so there's a change in the frequency of the ground clutter from the airborne movement — I have methods for controlling that as well. It turns out I just cannot see these low-flying very low radar cross-section targets.

So now I have the problem of drones that can home. So if we look at slide 21, this is actually an American drone which uses the Iranian design because the Iranian design is perfect. So Iran can take credit for designing the perfect drone that everybody uses. This is a Shahed — I have it incorrectly marked. It's a Gen 2 version — is a Russian drone which has an airframe exactly like the original Iranian design. The Shahed drone is the original Iranian drone and this is an American design which uses the same Iranian airframe.

So Iran is the inventor of the kamikaze drone that's running havoc with the United States and in Israel now and leading to Iran being the dominant military power now in this current war. So Iran's dominant military influence is now due to several factors. First of all, Iran has control of the Strait of Hormuz. So Iran has a stranglehold on the economy of the world and also the American and economies of all the other states in the world including the American economy. So Iran has been able to preferentially provide oil to countries that it has identified as non-hostile, not necessarily supporting it, but just non-hostile.

So Iran has very cleverly thought through the idea, well, we're not going to just stop it for everybody. If you're not hostile to us, we're going to leave it open. And by the way, Iranian oil — even the United States realizes that the Iranian oil is critical for keeping the total world economy from collapsing. So now Iran is selling oil not at the reduced rate of $45 per barrel because Iranian oil was sanctioned by the United States, but it's now able to sell its oil at $120 a barrel. So Iran is raking in money. It's almost a comedy that the Americans have now removed the sanctions on Iranian oil. So all of a sudden the Iranians are getting full price for their oil because of their control of the Strait of Hormuz. And of course they're able to provide oil to non-hostile states. That's a tremendously favorable situation for Iran in this war.

The next situation they have are these ballistic missiles which are doing tremendously more damage per impact than they could have been doing earlier. And the next problem is Iran has the precision information on the targets. So now some of this precision information could have come from other sources.

In the original attack in September of 2019 that Iran made against the Abqaiq Saudi Arabia oil fields. In the bottom of this drawing, I've shown a satellite photograph of one of these oil processing tanks. And notice that what is shown in the satellite photograph are the holes in the oil processing tank that were produced by Iranian cruise missiles hitting those tanks. The precision of those holes is to meters. It's incredible how accurately the Iranian cruise missiles were able to hit these processing tanks.

Well, I don't know exactly what specific technology the Iranians used, but I can give you a speculation. If you look at the next slide, what the Iranians could have done is taken a satellite photograph of those tanks. In other words, they didn't have to have help from China or Russia. All they needed was a commercial photograph of those tanks. And in the upper left corner here, what they did is a cross. So the cruise missiles have an image of what they're looking for. Here's the image. It's my hand. And now what I have is the image and what I do is I cross the image with the image of my hand. I do it electronically. So when I somehow come across — when I cross — all the light gets passed through, none of the light gets passed through when I'm not lined up properly. So you see these three peaks. This is a simulation I actually did. It's a guess.

And what I've done, if you look to the right, I've shown you the peaks of the cross-correlation. And then in the bottom image, I show you the peaks of the cross-correlation overlaid on the targets. So this would have could have been done with satellite imagery and cross-correlation which we know the Iranians are well up to the job. So here's an example where it's not a drone necessarily being homed by what's called first-person guidance. In other words, it's not necessarily a drone operator who is sitting far away receiving a television signal from the drone, guiding the drone into the target. This could simply be a drone that is a robot and it knows enough to know where to go before it opens its eyes and takes a photograph of what's in front of it and cross-correlates that photograph with the target.

So here, so you can see, you begin to see how problematic these drones can begin to become because now I have the ability to do real-time homing. In other words, I'll talk about that. Well, I maybe talk about it in the next because we're now coming to the end of our time but the simplest conceptually simplest idea is that I have a drone and I'm looking through a television camera which means that the drone has to be sending a television image back to me. I might be thousands of kilometers away and then I have to give the drone signals. I don't need to do that in this particular case.

So now, so Iran, the indications are, at least to me, that Iran has drones that can do cross-correlation with images of the targets as well as drones that can home directly on signals from television cameras inside the drones. So that's a big innovation.

The availability of the Starlink system is now making it possible for Iran to use drones to do real-time homing on targets. So what happens? They get an image of the real-time situation. Actually, let's go to slide 33 just for a quick second.

If you look at slide 33, here's before and after. So I have an image of where these radar domes are. So I can destroy them in two ways. I can have an image of the radar dome and cross-correlate it with the drone as it comes in as I described earlier. Or I can simply home on the radar using a video camera. And the video camera can get its information from a Starlink signal.

Let me just show you. Let's go to slide 38 and I'll stop after this. You see this square on the right? This is a Starlink radio terminal. Let's just go one more slide forward. And here you see this little dog, cute little dog next to the Starlink communication system. That Starlink system can be — it weighs only a pound or two. Let's go one more slide. And there it is in a box that you can order. Notice the box has instructions for how to use it. You can go out — anyone can go out and order from Amazon for about $250 one of these things and you'll get it in the mail in a few days. And you can then modify that and put it on your drone if you know how to utilize the signal.

Now then you can send your drone out looking for a target and you can be using the drone communications through your Starlink satellite communication. Now let's say I'm the United States. I don't want anyone out there using that drone. Well, how am I supposed to know someone is out there using the drone? I've just sold them a terminal. The terminal has encrypted communications because that's the way you keep the signals secure for people who want to communicate just normally. So how do I know that it's someone with his drone flying around looking for something to hit? I have no way of knowing. So I have no way of selectively turning off the drone signal. All I can do is turn off all the Starlink satellites that are functioning over let's say the air base. But if I do that then I turn off all the Starlink communications that the air base is using. So I have a dilemma.

So the problem is very serious — the Starlink communication system. So now we see how the modern technologies — navigation technology, GPS, Chinese Beidou system that are widely available. These are very high technology systems but they're universally available. You don't need to understand the system. You don't need to be able to build it yourself. You just need access to its service. And so you have the service, you have the high precision real-time satellite imagery from the Russian and Chinese satellites and you're in business.

And you have Russia who has been attacked using American missiles given to Ukraine by the United States to attack Russia. And Russia has told the United States when the United States did this: if you do this, we at some future time will be giving information to someone else to attack you. You did this in Kursk and you gave the Ukrainians all this information. You gave them satellite surveillance information. You gave them communication information so the drones can attack our strategic bombers, for example. Okay. You want to play that game? That's fine because we're going to attack your AWACS. We're going to see that the Iranians know where those AWACS are parked. We're going to see that the Iranians know where the radars are and we're going to give them that information and they're going to have the navigation resources that they can just buy commercially and good luck. And now Iran is benefiting from these services and so incidentally are China and Russia because they're in the mode of — they want those countries — they want Iran to prevail and Iran I think will prevail in this.

I think the war is going to get worse and worse and worse for the Americans. And my gravest concern is that as Israel gets into more and more desperate situation, they might be foolish enough to use nuclear weapons. And the American political decision makers better make sure that Israel understands that they are on their own if they ever use nuclear weapons against anyone. They are absolutely on their own forever, completely. Because I'm concerned that this crazy guy Netanyahu who definitely has the mentality that Adolf Hitler had doesn't do something to bring down Israel along with everybody else. And Adolf Hitler would have brought down Germany. We have every reason to believe he would have brought down Germany if he had the ability to do it with nuclear weapons because he was suicidal and it would have only taken an intervention of the kind that Albert Speer did. Albert Speer was his architect. He told Albert who was running the German economy to destroy Germany and Speer failed to follow his order because he didn't want Germany completely destroyed. Otherwise, Germany would have been destroyed if that order was carried out.

So you have to hope that either Netanyahu is not as crazy as he looks or that people who he would order to do the job will refuse to do the job. And I sincerely hope that the kind of Israelis that I know are in the loop and they would refuse to do the job because they're smart enough to know that it would be the end of Israel along with a lot of other things. But I don't know how much control has been taken by this man, this lunatic.

So anyway, it's all there.

**Host:** Yeah, thank you. Thank you so much. You're in Germany. I hope you enjoy your trip to Germany.

**Ted Postol:** I enjoyed myself in Germany.

**Host:** Yeah, see you soon.

**Ted Postol:** You take care and nice talking to you. I hope it's been of some interest to your audience.

**Host:** It was amazing. It was amazing. Thank you. Bye-bye
